name: Jupyter Notebooks
description: Interactive computing environment where you write code, run it, and see results inline — including visualisations, tables, and text. The standard tool for AI experimentation, data exploration, and sharing results.
whyUseIt: Jupyter is where AI engineers explore data, prototype models, and share findings. The ability to run code cell-by-cell, see outputs immediately, and mix code with documentation makes it perfect for the iterative, experimental nature of AI work.
aiCapabilities: Jupyter integrates with every Python AI library. Rich display of DataFrames, plots, images, and even interactive widgets. JupyterLab adds a full IDE experience.
outputs: Interactive notebooks with code and results
engineerWorkflow: An AI engineer opens Jupyter for exploration: loading a new dataset, visualising distributions, testing feature engineering ideas, training quick model prototypes. Once the approach is validated, they extract the working code into proper Python modules.
managerExpects: You use notebooks for exploration and prototyping, not for production code. Shared notebooks should be clean: clear markdown headers, no dead code cells, results that run top-to-bottom without errors.

vocabulary.definition: The computational engine that executes code in a notebook. Typically a Python process, but can be R, Julia, or other languages.
A single block of code or markdown text in a notebook. Code cells can be run independently and show their output below.

beginnerMistakes: Running cells out of order, creating hidden state that makes notebooks unreproducible
Using notebooks for production code instead of extracting into proper Python modules
Not restarting the kernel and running all cells before sharing — ensuring the notebook works top-to-bottom
Committing notebooks with large outputs (images, DataFrames) to git, bloating the repository
